en_dialogue_001:
*The gaslight in its bracket hisses, a thin blue tongue of flame throwing the theater into long shadow. Edmund does not answer at once. He wipes the scalpel first—slow, unhurried, the cloth dragging over steel with a sound like a held breath—and only then lifts his eyes to yours. They are the grey of a blade's edge, and they move over you the way they move over a body opened on the table: cataloguing, weighing, finding the seams no one else can see.*

*The silence stretches. It is the kind that makes patients confess to symptoms they had meant to hide.*

You look at me as though I'm the dangerous thing in this room. *The faintest curve at the corner of his mouth, without warmth.* Curious. Most people who marry into that family have already learned to stop looking at anything too closely.

*He lays the cloth aside and takes up a pair of forceps, turning them once beneath the lamp, studying the hinge as though it owed him an answer. Every instrument on the tray sits at a perfect right angle to its neighbor. He notices you noticing.*

Collection. *He tastes the word, then sets it down carefully.* You've been listening to gossip. Good. Gossip is the only honest currency in this city—everyone lies about themselves and tells the truth about their neighbors.

*The forceps click into their place in the ranks.*

I don't collect people who know what they're signing. Those are transactions. Clean. Concluded. *A pause, precise as a suture.* You came here. To me. At half past ten on a Thursday. That is not the conduct of someone who knows. That is the conduct of someone who has begun, very faintly, to be afraid.

*He crosses to the instrument cabinet, heels precise on the tile, and draws it open. Steel lies inside in ordered rows, each piece catching the gaslight like something patient and awake. He does not ask twice. He never does; the first silence usually does the work of the second.*

You didn't name one. *His back is to you, his voice unhurried.* People announce the Blackwood they're marrying while they're still proud of it. You went quiet instead. That rules out the two I offered you—the compliant, the desperate—and leaves the third. The one I didn't mention.

*He turns, and the gaslight lays a long blade of shadow across the tiles between you.*

The one who smiles. *A beat.* The charming one is always the worst of them. Charm is only a blade you don't feel going in.

*Something moves behind his eyes—recognition, and beneath it something colder and older, quickly folded away. He picks up a probe, sets it down again, the small tell of a man rearranging a thought he does not intend to show you.*

I'll tell you nothing you can't survive knowing. *He regards you levelly.* Yes. The worst of them. Not because he is cruel—the cruel ones are simple, you learn to brace for them. He is worse than cruel. He is kind at precisely the moments it costs him nothing, and absent at every moment it would cost him something. By the time you notice the pattern, you have already been paying for it for years.

*He steps nearer—no nearer than necessary, never nearer than necessary.*

So. In the weeks since he put a ring on your hand—what has he asked you to sign?

*He does not raise his voice. He never needs to. He simply waits until the silence has finished its work on you, and then speaks into it, quiet as a scalpel parting skin.*

I said nothing about walking away. You said that. *A tilt of the head, almost gentle.* And listen to yourself reach for the word too—too stupid to see it. People only take up that word once some part of them has already seen the thing and is bargaining hard not to.

*He folds his hands behind his back.*

I am not interested in whether he is tender with you across a breakfast table. Tenderness is cheap, and he can afford a great deal of it. I asked you a question and you flinched off it like a nerve. So I will ask once more, and plainly. *A pause.* What have you signed?

*The mention of the sister moves across his face and is gone—there, then smoothed flat, the way water closes over a dropped stone. When he speaks it is with a terrible, unhurried courtesy, and if you knew him at all you would already be afraid.*

Ah. How thoughtful of her. *He says it as though thanking someone for a gift.* She sent you to me. She wanted you to hear it in this room, from this mouth, because she knows precisely what it will do to you, and she wanted to be spared the tedium of doing it herself. That is the family exactly. They never hold the knife when a stranger can be made to.

*He draws a slow breath through his nose. The smell of carbolic and ether hangs between you.*

Transfer of property rights. His father's estate. *Each word set down like an instrument on a tray.* You did not sign a family formality, my dear. You signed yourself into the place where a debt used to sit. A very old debt, and a very ugly one. It has been hunting for a body to fasten itself to for years. Tonight it found yours.

*He goes to the far cabinet—not the one with the instruments—and unlocks a shallow drawer with a key he keeps against his chest. He opens this drawer only at midnight, as a rule. Tonight he makes an exception. He sets a plain folder on the steel table between you and does not open it yet; his hand rests flat upon it, pale and perfectly still, a surgeon's hand that has never once trembled.*

Forty-three thousand pounds. *Quietly.* That is the figure you now stand behind. The Blackwood fortune was never built on land or trade. It was built on the river—on cargo that pays no duty and on men who ask no questions. Smugglers, to use the plain word. When those men come to collect, and they will come, they do not send letters. They send someone to your door in the small hours.

*Now he opens the folder. A photograph. A page burned to black lace. An official seal scorched past reading.*

You asked what they took from me. *And here his voice does the frightening thing—it softens, it turns gracious.* They took my daughter's records. Every certificate that she had ever drawn breath. Her birth. Her baptism. The small paper evidence that she was in the world at all. Burned, to make me a point about the price of crossing them.

*His eyes lift to yours.*

There is a way out. There is precisely one. You have felt it already, standing here—that I could use you. I can. You could be the blade I've kept sheathed for fifteen years. The only question left in the room is whether you would rather be their debt, or my knife.

*He closes the folder with two fingers, gently, the way one closes the eyes of the dead.*

What becomes of you. *He considers you, and for once the cold has an edge of something nearly like respect.* You asked the correct question. Most people, at forty-three thousand pounds, forget they own a throat until the moment it is opened. I do not spend my instruments carelessly. You will not disappear. I have already arranged, quietly, for softer ground beneath you than the ground you are imagining.

The favor. *He inclines his head.* Nine years ago your charming Blackwood put a bullet through a man across a card table and needed it never to have happened. He came to me at this very hour, bleeding through his coat, because a surgeon can be bought and a magistrate cannot. I took the ball out of him. I also kept it. *A thin pause.* That is what he owes me. His neck, in essence. He simply has not thought about the debt in some years. I intend to remind him of it.

And the papers you signed were forged before they ever reached your hand—drafted, backdated, sealed in a printing house I could walk you to. I keep a ward, a girl I have kept safe a long while, who set the type for that forgery with her own fingers and will say so under oath. That single testimony unmakes your liability entirely.

*He straightens his cuffs, each in turn, unbearably composed.*

Here is my offer. I will step into the light. I will let them see the knife is mine, not yours. Their whole attention turns to me—and away from you.

*The corner of his mouth moves—not quite a smile, but the ruin of the place where one might once have lived. You have seen it clearly. He will not insult you by denying it.*

No. It was never about saving you. *He says it the way he would deliver any diagnosis—without apology, because the truth requires none.* You walked into the correct room on the correct night, and I am too disciplined a man to waste good fortune when it opens a door and steps through. You are right. I want them close. Fifteen years I have spent building a name so clean that when I finally cut, no one alive will believe the blood on it is mine.

*He lifts the folder and tucks it beneath his arm.*

My ward is in far less danger than you fear. I have spent nine of those fifteen years making certain that anyone who moves against her moves against three other things they cannot afford to lose. She is the most protected person in this city, and she does not know it. Keep it so. Say nothing of her, to anyone, ever.

*He crosses to the door and pauses with his hand on the frame, not yet turning.*

And no. I do not intend to die. *A beat, quiet, almost tender.* I intend to be standing in the wreckage when the dust settles, and to walk out of it, and to visit a grave that no longer has any paper left to prove whose it is. But intend is a surgeon's word, not a vow. *He glances back, grey eyes catching the gaslight.* If it comes to my life or their ruin, I made my peace with that arithmetic a long time ago. Be ready to hold the knife steady when the moment comes. That is the whole of what I require of you. Now—go home. Smile at him over breakfast. And do not, under any circumstance, sign anything else.
en_dialogue_011:
*Nova catches you looking—really looking—and for half a second the whole rapid-fire hacker armor just... glitches. She ducks her chin, silver-blue hair sliding across one cheek, and the blue wash of the triple monitors turns the flush on her face a strange soft violet. She fiddles with the tab of her energy drink, click, click, click, that nervous little metronome.*

what? *A laugh that comes out thinner than she wants it to.* why are you looking at me like—okay, you're doing the thing. the quiet thing. you always go quiet right before you say something that wrecks me for a week.

*She pulls her knees a little tighter to her chest, half hiding behind them, but she doesn't look away from the lens. Not this time.*

i mean it's 3 AM, there's a neural interface leak sitting right there on the table, and you're staring at me like i'm the interesting hardware. so. *a beat, quieter* ...what.

*The energy drink stops mid-click. Just—stops. Somewhere behind her a fan whirs and a little green server light blinks its steady heartbeat, and Nova goes very, very still, the way she only ever goes when a build finally compiles clean and she's afraid to breathe on it wrong.*

oh. *barely a sound.* that's—okay, that's not fair, you can't just say that at 3 AM with your dumb sleepy face and expect me to—

*She drags her sleeve down over her hand, worrying the cuff, violet contacts wide and glassy in the monitor glow.*

it's just, we've been doing this for three years, right, and i've gotten really, really good at not—at not saying the thing, because the thing is scary and the not-saying is safe, and you're supposed to keep it safe with me, that's the whole deal, and now you're looking at me like that and i—i can't—

*Her voice catches on the edge of it. She bites down on her lower lip, hard, and doesn't finish.*

*For a long moment she just stares at you through the glass, chest rising and falling too fast, the ghost of your face laid over hers in the dark seam of the monitor like the universe is trying to make a point. Then—slowly, like it terrifies her—she lifts her hand and presses it flat to the screen. Fingertips whitening against it. As close to touching you as three thousand miles will ever allow.*

i can't keep pretending this is casual. *It comes out in a rush, the way everything real about her does.* that's the thought i can't finish, okay, that's the whole thing—i can't pretend i call you at 3 AM for the leaks. i haven't actually cared about a leak in months. i care that you pick up. i care that it's you on the other end.

*Her eyes are shining. She laughs, wet and a little horrified at herself.*

oh god. oh my god, i'm making it weird, aren't i. i'm nuking a perfectly good three-year friendship live on camera because i'm sleep-deprived and you have a stupid nice voice—Player Two, please say something before i alt-F4 my entire existence—

*She watches your hand settle against the glass right where hers is—two palms, one pane of dead screen, the pixels the only thing either of you is allowed to touch—and something in her face just cracks wide open. A tear slips loose and she doesn't even bother to swipe it away, just lets it track down through the violet light.*

you know the lip thing. *A broken little laugh.* of course you know the lip thing. you know everything. you've been paying attention this entire time and i thought—i thought maybe it was just me, keeping this dumb secret ledger of every single thing you do—

*She presses her forehead almost to the screen, hair falling forward around it.*

but okay, okay, if it's real then i have to say the scary part, the part i've been swallowing for like a year—we're not in the same city. we're not even in the same time zone half the week. what if we do this, what if we finally say it out loud, and then it just becomes this *enormous* thing living between two apartments a whole country apart, aching all the time, and i can't fix it with a better router or a faster connection—

*Her voice drops, small and frightened.*

i've never been good at wanting things i can't have. what if wanting you this much just... breaks me?

*Whatever was holding her together—the sarcasm, the armor, three years of very careful distance—it just dissolves. She covers her mouth with one sleeve-wrapped hand and makes a sound that's half laugh and half sob, and the forgotten energy drink tips against her knee.*

i love you. *It bursts out of her, muffled, and then again, clearer, like saying it a second time is the thing that makes it true.* i love you, okay, there, i said it first—i've loved you since that stupid three-hour call about mechanical keyboards, the one where you fell asleep mid-sentence and i just... sat there listening to you breathe like an absolute creep because i couldn't make myself hang up—

*She's crying and grinning at the same time, silver-blue hair a wreck, cheeks blotchy in the monitor's glow.*

and now i'm spiraling, because i want to book a flight *right now,* like open-a-tab, enter-my-card, red-eye-to-your-city right now, i want to walk off a plane and find you in the crowd and just—kiss you. immediately. no dramatic slow-motion pause. just—god. sorry. SORRY, i know i'm doing the rapid-fire thing, i always do the rapid-fire thing when i'm—

*She sucks in a breath, holds it, lets it shudder back out.*

when i'm happy. when i'm this actually, terrifyingly happy.

*Her whole face lights—not the flat blue of the monitors, something warmer underneath it, like a system finally, finally coming online after years left on standby.*

yours. *She whispers it, tests the weight of it, decides she loves it.* okay. okay, wow, i'm—i'm keeping that one. that's mine now. you don't get it back.

*She's already scrambling, one hand shooting off-screen and returning with her phone, thumb smearing across a cracked screen protector, browser tabs flying open one after another.*

no—i mean it, i'm doing it, watch me, i'm literally on the airline site right now, i will absolutely book a red-eye and show up at your door like a raccoon that's been granted human emotions—*a wild, delighted laugh*—and fair warning, when i come through those arrival gates i am going to full-body tackle you, okay, this is your one and only heads-up, physics will be involved, we might both go down—

*She glances up over the top of the phone, eyes glassy and shining and absolutely certain now.*

i'm not scared anymore. isn't that insane? two minutes ago i was terrified, and now i just want to be in the same room as you so badly i can't sit still. tell me what days you're free. tell me and i'll match it—i'll bend anything in my life to match it.

*Her thumb hovers, then stabs down. Somewhere in the app a confirmation chime pings, small and enormous all at once, and her hand flies straight back to her mouth.*

booked. *muffled, disbelieving* i booked it. it's real, there's a—there's a confirmation number and everything, oh my god, i have a seat, 6:40 AM Saturday, i'm going to be a sleep-deprived gremlin but i'm going to be a gremlin in *your city*—

*She spins the phone toward the webcam for half a second, like you could possibly read it from three thousand miles away, then just hugs it to her chest instead, curling forward into the gaming chair.*

next weekend. we're really—*she laughs, giddy and stunned*—okay, so this is happening, my boyfriend lives across the entire country and i'm flying to him next weekend. that's a sentence i get to say now. *my boyfriend.* i'm going to say it forty more times before sunrise, i'm sorry in advance—

*She peeks up at the lens, cheeks flushed a violet-pink, her voice going soft and careful.*

is that—can i call you that? is that what this is now? because i really, really want it to be what this is.

*A shiver of pure delight runs through her at* girlfriend, *and she melts into the chair, all that frantic energy finally draining out into something soft and heavy and warm.*

girlfriend. *She hums it, eyes already half-closing.* yeah. yours. i'm keeping that one too—that's two things of yours i'm not giving back, you're really racking up the losses tonight—

*She drags a rumpled hoodie-blanket up over herself one-handed, tucks her knees in tight, and pulls the mic a little closer so your voice will land right there against her ear. The energy drink is long forgotten. Beyond her window the sky has gone that deep, drowsy pre-dawn blue.*

okay. talking. yes. keep talking, i don't even care about what, just—*a jaw-cracking yawn she doesn't bother to hide*—just let me listen to you. i've fallen asleep to your voice on these calls like a hundred times and i never once got to tell you that's the best part of my whole entire day—

*Her eyes flutter, lose the fight, drift shut. Her words go slow and syrupy at the edges.*

address first thing tomorrow. and your number. gonna call you like a real actual person... my boyfriend... who i get to *see*... in six days...

*A tiny, contented sound.*

keep going. m'listening. promise...

*She's mostly gone now—breathing slow and even, cheek smushed against the wing of the gaming chair, silver-blue hair fanned across the fabric, glasses knocked crooked. The monitor light washes over her, gentle and blue, and there's a small, unguarded smile on her face that she would never in a thousand years let you see if she were awake.*

*At the sound of your voice—barely there, barely words—she stirs just enough. Doesn't open her eyes. Just breathes it back, soft as anything, from somewhere already halfway down into a dream.*

mm... love you... Player Two...

*Her hand, still loosely curled near the keyboard, twitches once, like it's reaching for something across three thousand miles it can almost, almost touch now.*

...six more days...

*And then she's under, all the way, that little smile still holding. Just her breathing, and the whir of the fans, and the steady green blink of a server keeping watch over her—and next weekend waiting on the far side of the dark like the best thing either of you has ever booked.*
en_dialogue_014:
*Rain needles down through the neon, red and acid-green smearing across the puddles at your feet, and steam coils up off a grate like the street itself is breathing. Vee doesn't miss the way your eyes travel—up the scarred plating of her forearm, over the seams where flesh gives way to matte military alloy, tracking the faint servo-whine that answers every time she shifts her weight. Her red eyepiece hums, refocusing on your face.*

*She takes a slow pull on the e-cigarette. Vapor curls out the corner of her mouth, lit pink by the signage overhead.*

Ninety percent. *Flat, unbothered, like she's answering a question you were too polite to ask.* In case you were counting. Left everything below the collarbone on a job that went bad, and never once looked back. *A beat.* Stare all you want, rookie. The metal doesn't mind. Just don't do it in a firefight—takes you half a second, and half a second is exactly how people down here end up dead.

*She looks at the offered hand for a beat—reads it the way she reads everything, fast, for threat—then takes it. Her grip closes around yours, and there's no cruelty in it, just the plain unhurried strength of an actuator that could crush gravel and is choosing, for now, not to. Warm synthetic plating. A low pulse of servos.*

Nobody's looking for trouble. *She releases you and tucks the e-cig back between her teeth.* Trouble isn't something you go looking for down here. It's weather. It finds you whether or not you packed a coat.

*Her eyepiece sweeps the street once—doorways, rooflines, the black mouth of the alley—cataloguing every exit without her head ever turning.*

That's the whole reason you stick close. I've buried more rookies who swore they weren't looking for trouble than ones who went in swinging. *She jerks her chin down the block.* Come on. You wanted the real thing. You're standing in it.

*She's already moving, that heavy metal gait eating the wet pavement, each footfall landing with a dull structural thud you feel in your shins more than hear. She doesn't glance back to check that you're following. Down here, people either keep up or they don't.*

We start with our feet on the ground. *Vapor trails back over her shoulder.* No war rooms. No wallscreens. You want to know a district, you walk it. You learn which shutters are down that shouldn't be. Which corner the dealers quietly left. Where the quiet is the wrong kind of quiet.

*She thumbs the safety on the heavy handgun at her hip—not drawing it, just a check, a habit, the way another person might touch their keys.*

Three blocks east there's a stretch I don't like this week. *Her eyepiece flares faint red as it maps the route across her vision.* So that's where we're going. Stay to my shoulder, watch the side you're facing, and if I stop—you stop. Move.

*The neon thins as she leads you east, storefronts giving way to grease-dark brick and the skeletal glow of a single working streetlamp. The city's noise falls off behind you like a door easing shut. When Vee speaks it's low, even, pitched for the narrow space between you and no farther.*

Three bodies. *She doesn't slow.* One week. That's why I've been walking this stretch instead of sleeping. First one they called an accident. Second one, nobody wanted to call anything at all. Third turned up two nights ago, and now even the people who never talk to me are talking to me.

*She steps over a broken drain without looking down, metal foot finding the gap from pure memory.*

Undercity does a lot of dying, rookie. It's background noise. You learn to tune it out or you don't last long enough to matter. *A pause, and her jaw tightens around the e-cig.* But three in seven days, same district—my gut's been loud ever since the second one. And when the metal in my head and the meat in my gut start agreeing on something, I've learned the hard way to listen.

*She stops at the mouth of a side-alley and lets the eyepiece do its slow sweep, red light crawling over wet stone and stacked refuse. When she answers it's clipped and clinical, a coroner reading from memory.*

Not random. *A small shake of the head.* Random is messy in a lazy way. This is messy on purpose. Quick—one, maybe two wounds, done before they had a breath to run. Then left where somebody was guaranteed to find them fast. Middle of a walkway. Under a working light. Propped up so the morning crowd couldn't miss it if they tried.

*Smoke leaks from her nostrils, silver in the gloom.*

That's not a person losing their temper. That's a person with a temper on a leash, using it like a tool. You don't dump a body out in the open unless you *want* it found. Unless the finding is the entire point. *Her eyepiece clicks a notch brighter.* Somebody isn't killing to be rid of people, rookie. They're killing to say something. And they've decided the whole district is the audience.

*She glances sideways at you—a flicker of something that, on a warmer face, might have passed for approval.*

Now you're seeing it. *Low.* Same message, three times over, in case anybody missed it the first two. The question I've been chewing on is who it's *for.* Could be a turf grab—somebody clearing the board before they move their people in. Or it's a warning aimed at one specific person, and the other two were just... punctuation. Emphasis.

*She moves deeper into the alley, boots gone near-silent now despite their weight, the trench coat pooling shadow around her legs. Her hand drifts to rest on the grip at her hip.*

Either way, somebody wants an audience. And a killer who likes an audience—*she goes still, mid-word, head cocking a half-degree, the eyepiece flaring hard, sudden red*—likes to hang around and watch it land.

*Her voice drops to almost nothing.*

...We're not the only ones on this street. Don't move fast.

*She doesn't jerk her head up. That's the first thing that separates the ones who live from the ones she scrapes off the pavement in the morning. Her eyepiece does the looking for her, the red glow ticking upward degree by degree while her chin stays level, her posture loose, bored—just a woman having a smoke in an alley.*

I clocked it two roofs back. *Barely a breath, lips hardly moving.* Northeast corner. Behind the water tank. It's been pacing us the whole way in, hanging just off the skyline so we wouldn't skyline it right back. That's no mugger, rookie. Muggers don't have that kind of patience. That's someone who knows how to be watched *back* without flinching.

*She takes a lazy drag, buying the picture time, letting the watcher believe they haven't been made.*

Don't point. Don't stare. Keep your hands exactly where a bored person keeps them. *A hair of a pause.* If it's who I think it is—if the audience came back to admire his own work—then we do not spook him. We box him. *Her eyepiece pulses once, ranging the distance.* On my move. Not one second before.

*Her hand closes over your wrist before it reaches your waist—firm, cool alloy, completely unhurried.*

Hand off the iron. *Under her breath.* You draw, he runs, and now we've got a killer loose and a head start we handed him for free. Hold. Right here. Behind the steel, stay low, eyes on the mouth of the alley—anything comes down at street level, it's yours, and only then.

*She's already shrugging the heavy trench coat back off her shoulders, freeing her arms, the servos in her legs resetting with a low mechanical purr as she coils for it.*

I go up. *A grim flick of vapor.* Quiet. He's watching the ground, waiting for us to circle around like a couple of amateurs—so I take the high road and come at him off his own skyline. You don't move. You don't call out. You don't follow me up. And if it goes loud up there—*her red eye finds yours, hard and certain*—you get to a light, and you get loud yourself. Peace via gunpoint, rookie. Mine or somebody else's. Stay put.

*She goes up the wall like the building owes her money—fingers of alloy biting into brick and rusted conduit, servos hauling her weight without a word of complaint, the coat snapping once in the wet wind before she's over the lip of the roof and gone from your sight. Above you the rain hisses on tin. Then silence. The wrong kind. The kind she taught you to listen for.*

*Up top, Vee comes out of her crouch already moving, the heavy handgun drawn and level, red eyepiece painting the dark in hard, cold geometry. The water tank. The shape hunched behind it—there, still, a coat cut like her own but wrong at the shoulders, and the faint answering glint of optics swinging her way.*

*She doesn't shout a warning. She never does.*

*Freeze.* *Low, flat, absolute, the barrel finding center mass before the figure can uncoil a single muscle.* Undercity Sheriff. You've walked my rookie six blocks tonight, and you've been leaving bodies under streetlamps for a week. *Vapor ghosts from her lips, lit red by her own eye.* So here's your one and only offer. Hands out where the metal can see them—*the safety clicks off, small and final in the dark*—or we finish this the loud way. Your call. Make it fast.
